title: Urine on Silk — Cool Water + NSD, Acid Rinse
stainChemistry: Urine is urea, uric acid (oxidizes yellow), and ammonia. Acidic early, becomes alkaline over time. Cannot use strong alkali on silk. Cannot saturate delicate fiber. Cool detergent + acid rinse is safest approach.
whyThisWorks: Detergent suspends urine residues. Mild acid stabilizes any remaining uric acid and prevents oxidation.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse immediately with cool water. Urine is water-soluble; immediate rinse removes much of the stain. Do NOT use hot water.

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD (1 teaspoon NSD in 1 cup cool water), minimal saturation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply dilute detergent sparingly from back side. Do NOT saturate silk. Allow 5-10 minutes. Blot gently as residue lifts.
Rinse using barely damp cloth. Do NOT saturate. Work quickly—silk weakens when wet.

spottingProtocol.agent: Mild acid rinse in 1 cup cool water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply vinegar solution. Acid stabilizes any remaining uric acid and prevents oxidative yellowing. Be very gentle—do not squeeze.
Rinse gently with barely damp cloth. Remove all vinegar. Be very careful—silk is delicate when wet.

homeSolutions.agent: Urine on silk: speed is everything, and enzymes are off-limits
homeSolutions.instruction: Fresh urine on silk is recoverable at home. Dried or set urine on silk is usually NOT — enzymes (the main home tool for uric acid) digest silk protein along with urine protein, so the most effective tool is banned on this fiber. Your home window is roughly the first hour. After that, or if the yellow has set, take it to a silk specialist. Acting fast is more useful than acting strong.

Silk bans: NO enzymes (digest silk), no hot water, no chlorine bleach, no alkaline (pH > 7 weakens silk), no wringing or scrubbing. Protein bans: no hot water (sets protein permanently), no alcohol (sets protein beyond pro recovery). Home toolbox here: cool water only, 1:1 dilute white vinegar (60-sec ceiling), silk-safe detergent (Eucalan or Soak) for the final cycle.

Dab cool water on an inside seam, wait 60 seconds, blot with a white cloth. If the seam loses color, stop — go to step 7. If clean, press a white paper-towel stack straight down on the stain. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside-in until the towel stops picking up fluid. Do NOT wipe or rub.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool water back-flush — gentle, not a soak
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn the garment inside out. Place a clean white towel underneath. Dab cool water on the BACK of the stain with a cotton ball, letting the underside towel catch runoff. Replace the underside towel as it absorbs yellow. Work 30–60 seconds. The goal is to flush surface urine OUT the way it came in. Silk + saturation + friction = felting risk; keep wetness localized.

Mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cool water. Press a cotton swab onto the stain. 60 SECONDS MAX — set a timer. Rinse with cool water immediately. Vinegar does two things here: breaks down uric acid salts (the only home tool on silk that touches them) AND keeps silk's natural pH. Over-dwell weakens silk fibroin.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never full-strength vinegar on silk. Never combine with bleach on the same pass.

homeSolutions.instruction: Hand-wash the area (or the whole garment if it fits a basin) with a drop of Eucalan or Soak in cool water. GENTLE swishing only — no wringing, no twisting, no scrubbing. Rinse in cool water. Press between dry white towels to lift moisture. Lay flat on a fresh towel, reshape while damp. Air-dry at room temperature, out of direct sun. No heat source.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — silk + urine is a narrow home fight
homeSolutions.instruction: If the patch test failed, the urine was older than 1–2 hours, dried, or heat-set, take to a silk specialist (call ahead — not every dry cleaner treats silk). Tell them: "Urine on silk, age [x], treated at home with cool water + 1:1 vinegar (60-sec dwell) + silk-safe detergent. One round, no enzymes, no alkaline, no heat." Pros have protein digestants formulated for silk + reducing agents for the yellow that home vinegar can't finish.

materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — silk dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
NEVER use hot water—spreads urine and weakens silk.
NEVER use alkaline cleaners—urine turns alkaline over time; don't accelerate this.
escalation: If detergent and acid treatment don't resolve urine staining, call professional. Aged uric acid stains may require specialist treatment.

household.name: Seventh Generation Free & Clear (diluted)
household.note: For urine residue suspension. Use diluted and minimally.

household.name: White Vinegar (5% acetic acid)
household.note: Critical final step. Stabilizes uric acid, prevents yellowing. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

professional.name: Specialty Silk Urine Treatment
professional.note: For aged uric acid oxidation on silk.

customerExplanation: Urine is mostly water-soluble urea plus uric acid that yellows over time. We rinse immediately with cool water, use gentle detergent to suspend residues, then apply mild acid to prevent the uric acid from oxidizing further.
commonMistakes: Using hot water—spreads urine and weakens silk.
Not treating quickly—uric acid oxidizes over hours and becomes much harder to remove.
Skipping acid rinse—leaves uric acid unstable and prone to yellowing.
scienceNote: Urine's uric acid (C5H4N4O3) oxidizes gradually through air exposure, forming darker colored xanthine and related compounds. Acid (pH 3-4) stabilizes uric acid in its less-oxidative state. This is why timing is critical—fresh urine rinses easily; aged uric acid oxidation products are much harder to remove.
callPro: If cool water, detergent, and acid treatment don't fully resolve urine staining, call professional. Aged oxidized uric acid may require specialized treatment.
solventNote: Professional shops: Cool water rinse immediately (timing critical). Detergent for residue suspension. Acid rinse essential to stabilize uric acid. Minimal saturation on silk. Do NOT use hot water or alkaline. For aged oxidized stains, assessment before treatment recommended.
aftercare: Lay flat or hang very loosely. Never wring. As it dries, monitor the area.
